author: @stevecummins324
text:
Ideal angle would be at right angle to sun's Ray. Will vary with latitude, time of day, and also date in year.

May be possible to make an automatic tracker out of thermally sensitive material. Wax struts like used for greenhouse automatic vent, or even rubber bands which weaken their pull when heated. Arrange so pair of rubber bands tilt in opposite directions, and "window" to individual rubber band looking off axis, so that rubber band warmed more when that rubber band needs to relax more.

author: @TheLastEpiscopalian
text: Hi Robert great vid and long time subscriber. Can you pretty please do a video on sonic resonation used to cut stone. Much like playing the exact frequency of a wine glass into itself via a speaker, the wine glass shatters. If you bang a bell (without its clapper) you can make another identical one resonate. And with the right engineering can sharpen the end of the bell to cut through stone as it vibrates. Needs a bit of fiddeling and loud resonating power like 100s of bells. But the idea is there.

author: @newmonengineering
text: I have a solar water heater on the roof with the same tubes. If you let it, it will definitely boil water in a very short time. Mide definitely made an impact on the electric bill. It is significantly hot and it only uses a tiny 30watt pump to recirculate the water to the roof and back. I think they are the most efficient currently for heating water. It even heats while it's raining.

author: @brianperrett4429
text:
With the high temperatures we are experiencing in the UK could you do a video on home made evaporative coolers.....also known as swamp coolers?
It would be good to see what a well designed and built homemade swamp cooler can do ...and they could be just what people need to get some relief from the heat.  
Much cheaper to run than aircon.
I hope you take a look at them. Loads on Youtube but I would like to see your take on them.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @totherarf
text:
It works. It is free to use. What is there not to like (unless you sell electricity)?

It would seem that you gain a vast amount of efficiency by heating direct rather than generating power to heat with.
It seems like nearly every country that gets decent sunshine has bits of kit on their roofs to heat their water. I have heard of using the effect to heat swimming pools (outdoor garden types, not municipal) with little more than a coil of PEX pipe and a box all painted black with a perspex cover! I feel that with decent insulation we should be doing more like that here in the UK.

Likewise with passive solar heating using cans stacked together to form a matrix again in a box, painted black and a perspex front on but this time using air directly heated to warm houses!

Maybe we will see an increase in uptake when the next power price hike hits us?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yep - that's basically right mate - most folks won't do it until the price of energy is high enough

author: @CairnOfDunnCroftPermaculture
text: You can buy them with both ends open if you're prepared to buy in bulk from Asia. The advantage of both ends open of course is you can just run copper pipe through them, and have it thermosiphon from a hot water tank.

author: @brianoconnell3652
text: I have tinked with them. I use them to heat the greenhouse in the winter. Also with a small DC pump you can use it to heat your home and try and save on fuel costs. replies.author: @ricksanchez3176
replies.text:
Elevation, time of day, time of year, all going to come into play.  You can do a bunch of math, but the math will come out that if the shadow of the same length as the target, it's optimised.  
*If where the shadow is being cast is relatively close.
